questions.question: On a utility possibility frontier between Ava and Noah, what does a point inside the frontier indicate?

choices.A: The allocation is Pareto inefficient because at least one person can be made better off without making the other worse off.
choices.B: The allocation is Pareto efficient because utilities are positive.
choices.C: The allocation is unfair but always efficient.
choices.D: The allocation maximizes total surplus automatically.

questions.explanation: A point inside the frontier leaves feasible gains unexploited, so it is Pareto inefficient. A is correct. B ignores feasible improvements, C confuses fairness and efficiency, and D is unsupported.

questions.question: On a production possibilities frontier for apples and oranges, what does a point on the frontier represent in Pareto-efficiency terms if consumers value both goods?

choices.A: Productive efficiency, and possibly Pareto efficiency if the mix also matches consumers' marginal valuations.
choices.B: Pareto inefficiency because one good could be increased only by reducing the other.
choices.C: Guaranteed fairness between all consumers.
choices.D: Zero opportunity cost.

questions.explanation: Being on the PPF means no more of one good can be produced without less of another. With appropriate consumption allocation and marginal valuation conditions, it can be Pareto efficient. A is correct. B mistakes tradeoff for inefficiency, C confuses efficiency with equity, and D denies opportunity cost.

questions.question: What is the key limitation of Pareto efficiency as a welfare criterion?

choices.A: It does not rank distributions when helping one person requires hurting another, so highly unequal allocations can be Pareto efficient.
choices.B: It requires everyone to have equal income.
choices.C: It says only monopoly markets are efficient.
choices.D: It ignores whether resources are scarce.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Pareto efficiency rules out waste but does not address fairness or distribution. B is the opposite of the limitation, C is false, and D is false because scarcity is central to the concept.

questions.question: A school assigns all 20 pizza slices to one club while another club gets none. There is no way to give the second club a slice without taking one from the first. Which statement is correct?

choices.A: The allocation can be Pareto efficient even though it may seem unfair.
choices.B: The allocation is Pareto inefficient because it is unequal.
choices.C: The allocation is Pareto efficient only if both clubs receive 10 slices.
choices.D: The allocation is impossible because Pareto efficiency requires equality.

questions.explanation: A is correct. If all slices are allocated and transferring one slice helps one club only by hurting the other, the allocation can be Pareto efficient despite inequality. B, C, and D confuse efficiency with fairness.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes Pareto efficiency from equity?

choices.A: Pareto efficiency asks whether gains without losses are possible; equity asks whether the distribution is fair.
choices.B: Pareto efficiency and equity are identical concepts.
choices.C: Pareto efficiency requires equal outcomes; equity requires marginal cost pricing.
choices.D: Equity ignores distribution, while Pareto efficiency measures fairness.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Pareto efficiency is about absence of unexploited mutual gains, while equity concerns fairness. B collapses distinct ideas, C reverses them, and D reverses them.

questions.question: Demand is P=50-Q and social marginal cost is MC=10+Q. A policy sets Q=10. What deadweight loss exists relative to the Pareto efficient quantity?
questions.explanation: Efficient quantity solves 50-Q=10+Q, so Q*=20. At Q=10, MB=40 and MC=20, so the lost surplus triangle from 10 to 20 has base 10 and height 20: DWL=100. A is correct. B uses a rectangle, C doubles the rectangle, and D ignores underproduction.

questions.question: Demand for flu shots is P=80-Q, and private supply equals social marginal cost P=20+Q. There are no externalities or information problems. What competitive quantity is Pareto efficient according to the First Welfare Theorem logic?

choices.A: Q=30 because competitive equilibrium sets marginal willingness to pay equal to marginal cost.
choices.B: Q=60 because the demand intercept minus supply intercept is 60.
choices.C: Q=80 because all consumers with positive willingness to pay should receive shots.
choices.D: Q=0 because no allocation can be improved.

questions.explanation: Set 80-Q=20+Q, giving Q=30. With complete markets and no failures, competitive equilibrium is Pareto efficient. A is correct. B uses the price intercept gap as quantity, C ignores marginal cost, and D denies gains from trade.

questions.question: A competitive market has demand P=120-2Q and supply P=20+2Q. Assuming the First Welfare Theorem conditions hold, what is the efficient quantity and price?
questions.explanation: Set 120-2Q=20+2Q, so Q=25 and P=70. A is correct. B uses the intercept difference as quantity, C is an arithmetic error, and D does not equate demand and supply.

questions.question: A competitive market initially satisfies the First Welfare Theorem assumptions. A negative production externality appears. What changes?

choices.A: Competitive equilibrium may no longer be Pareto efficient because private supply lies below social marginal cost.
choices.B: The theorem becomes stronger because externalities increase trade.
choices.C: Nothing changes because competitive prices always include all costs.
choices.D: The market must shut down completely.

questions.explanation: A is correct. A negative externality violates a key condition because private decisions ignore external costs. B and C falsely assume external costs are internalized, and D overstates the conclusion.

questions.question: A competitive market has complete information. Then sellers gain private information about product defects that buyers cannot observe. How does this affect the First Welfare Theorem's applicability?

choices.A: It weakens applicability because asymmetric information can prevent prices from coordinating mutually beneficial trades efficiently.
choices.B: It strengthens applicability because sellers know more.
choices.C: It has no effect because information is never part of efficiency.
choices.D: It guarantees Pareto efficiency only if price rises.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Information asymmetry violates a standard condition for the First Welfare Theorem. B mistakes private knowledge for market-wide information, C ignores the theorem's assumptions, and D gives an unsupported price condition.

questions.question: On a supply-demand graph with no externalities, what graphical condition supports the First Welfare Theorem?

choices.A: The competitive intersection occurs where demand, representing marginal benefit, equals supply, representing marginal cost.
choices.B: The competitive quantity is where demand is highest.
choices.C: The competitive quantity is where supply is lowest.
choices.D: The competitive price is zero.

questions.explanation: A is correct. The theorem's intuition is that competitive equilibrium exhausts mutually beneficial trades by equating MB and MC. B and C focus on endpoints rather than marginal equality, and D is not required.

questions.question: If a competitive equilibrium quantity is shown where demand equals private supply, but social marginal cost lies above private supply, what does the graph imply?

choices.A: The First Welfare Theorem conditions fail because private supply does not reflect true social cost.
choices.B: The allocation is Pareto efficient because demand equals private supply.
choices.C: There is underproduction because social cost is higher.
choices.D: There is no deadweight loss because all trades are voluntary.

questions.explanation: A is correct. A wedge between private and social marginal cost indicates an externality, violating the theorem's assumptions. B ignores social cost, C reverses the usual overproduction result for negative externalities, and D ignores external harm.

questions.question: What does the First Welfare Theorem claim?

choices.A: Every competitive equilibrium is Pareto efficient under conditions such as no externalities, no public goods, complete markets, and sufficient information.
choices.B: Every Pareto efficient allocation is a competitive equilibrium with the current distribution of wealth.
choices.C: Markets always produce fair outcomes.
choices.D: Government intervention can never improve anyone's welfare.

questions.explanation: A is correct. B resembles the Second Welfare Theorem and also needs redistribution assumptions, C confuses efficiency with equity, and D overstates the theorem and ignores market failures.

questions.question: A market for used laptops has many buyers and sellers, but sellers know battery health and buyers cannot observe it before purchase. Which First Welfare Theorem assumption is most directly threatened?

choices.A: No information asymmetries.
choices.B: No fixed costs.
choices.C: Upward-sloping supply.
choices.D: Positive accounting profit.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Hidden battery quality creates asymmetric information. B is not a theorem condition, C is not the central issue, and D is irrelevant to Pareto efficiency.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes the First Welfare Theorem from the claim that markets are fair?

choices.A: The theorem is about Pareto efficiency, not equality or fairness of the resulting distribution.
choices.B: The theorem says everyone receives equal income in competitive equilibrium.
choices.C: The theorem says no one can be poor in a competitive market.
choices.D: The theorem applies only when government chooses the allocation.

questions.explanation: A is correct. The theorem rules out unexploited Pareto improvements under assumptions but does not judge distributional fairness. B and C falsely add equity claims, and D contradicts the market-equilibrium setting.

questions.question: Demand is P=100-Q. Private supply is P=20+Q, but production creates external cost of $20 per unit, so social marginal cost is P=40+Q. What is the deadweight loss at the unregulated competitive equilibrium?
questions.explanation: Private equilibrium: 100-Q=20+Q gives Q=40. Efficient quantity: 100-Q=40+Q gives Q=30. At Q=40, the wedge between SMC and demand is 20. DWL=0.5×10×20=100. A is correct. B uses a rectangle, C doubles the rectangle, and D uses the full external cost over all output.
